doctype.name: doctype
doctype.description: An ordered composition of blocks into a standalone document.

analysis.description: The main investigation — evidence and reasoning.

rubric.evidence-to-outcome: Walk the evidence and reasoning that connects the questions to the outcome.
rubric.sourced: Lean on the quotations/sources; real specifics, not vague claims.

context.description: What exists today and where the gap is.

rubric.state-then-gap: Describe the current state in prose, then name the specific gap this closes.
rubric.cite-real: Cite the real files/types that exist now.
rubric.prose: Enough prose to orient a reader, not just a file list.

criterion.description: One semantic rubric item the judge grades Rules of this type against.

rubric.judgment-only: A quality the mechanical guards cannot check, not a format rule.

decision.description: A settled choice carried in; do not re-litigate.

rubric.decision-and-why: State the decision and the one-line why.
rubric.closed-only: Closed choices only — genuinely open ones go in open-question.

expected-result.description: The hypothesis — what we expected to find before looking.

rubric.state-expectation: State the prior expectation plainly, so the outcome can confirm or upset it.
rubric.short: One short block; not a place for analysis.

open-question.description: A genuinely unresolved decision the reader must make.

rubric.question-and-options: State the question and the realistic options.
rubric.not-decided: If already decided, it is a decision, not an open question.

outcome.description: What was actually found — the conclusion.

rubric.answer-and-match: Answer the questions and say whether the result matched the expectation.
rubric.decisive: Concrete and decisive; name follow-ups only if real.

phase.description: One ordered, shippable step of the build.

rubric.reuse-first: Lead with what the step reuses before what it adds.
rubric.goal-and-done: The body states the goal and the done-when bar.
rubric.status: Carry `status`; for a blocked phase, list blockers as a sub-list.
rubric.caveat: Use `caveat` for a short qualifier on an otherwise-done phase.
rubric.min-two: At least two phases — a single step is not a plan.

question.description: A specific question the research sets out to answer.

rubric.one-question: One concrete, answerable question per member; phrase it as a question.
rubric.why-it-matters: Body says why it matters or scopes it; keep it tight.

quotation.description: A cited quote or source bearing on a question.

rubric.faithful-attributed: Quote or paraphrase faithfully; attribute it in `source`.
rubric.excerpt: Keep each to the load-bearing excerpt, not a wall.

rule.description: One guarantee — a behaviour or invariant — stated as the heading, justified by its Why.

rubric.guarantee-not-implementation: States a behaviour or invariant guaranteed, not how it is coded.
rubric.one-per-rule: Exactly one guarantee, not several bundled.
rubric.why-justifies: The Why gives the property at stake, not a restatement of the header.

scope.description: A bounded in/out list that draws the work boundary.

rubric.in-and-out: One "### In scope" member (what this builds) and one "### Out of scope" (owned elsewhere, with where).
rubric.real-bullets: Bullets, each a real capability — not a vague gesture.

summary.description: One-paragraph what-and-why at the very top.

rubric.outcome-first: Outcome-first — state the objective and what "done" means, concretely.
rubric.tight: One tight paragraph of real prose, not a bullet skeleton or a wall.

rubric.concrete-checks: Name concrete checks — build, tests, and at least one behaviour run end-to-end.
rubric.end-to-end: Not just "it compiles". One block, near the end.

feature-plan.description: Build or change a feature — objective, scope, ordered phases, verification.

rubric.coverage: Every load-bearing part of the dump has a block; nothing significant dropped.
rubric.right-type: Each piece sits in the right block type (settled → decision, open → open-question, steps → phase).
rubric.no-filler: No filler blocks — each carries real substance.
rubric.standalone: The doc stands alone — no references to the dump, prior chat, or earlier drafts.
rubric.concrete: Names real files and symbols, not vague gestures.
rubric.reuse-first: Each phase leads with what it reuses before what it adds.
rubric.open-lean: Open questions are genuinely open and carry a recommended lean.
rubric.verify-real: Verification runs a real end-to-end behaviour, not just build/typecheck.
rubric.prose: Prose is substantive where it matters; bold marks labels, not emphasis.

research.description: Investigate questions against sources and record what was found.

rubric.coverage: Every investigated question, key source, and the conclusion has a block.
rubric.right-type: Content sits in the right block (questions → question, sources → quotation, conclusion → outcome).
rubric.standalone: The doc stands alone — no references to the source, prior chat, or drafts.
rubric.sourced: Every claim ties to a cited source; no unsupported assertions.
rubric.headline-first: The summary leads with the finding, not a buried conclusion.
rubric.expectation-vs-outcome: The outcome says whether the result matched the expected-result.
rubric.prose: Prose is substantive; bold marks labels, not emphasis.

rubric.description: The per-test-type schema — names the type and carries the judge criteria its Rules are graded against.

rubric.criteria-cover: The criteria cover the semantic qualities a Rule of this type must have.
rubric.judgment-only: Criteria name qualities the mechanical guards cannot check.
rubric.named-type: The testType matches the Rulebook it governs.

rulebook.description: A test type's Rulebook — a list of Rules, each a guarantee proven by tests.

rubric.coverage: Every guarantee of this type has a Rule; nothing significant unstated.
rubric.right-type: Each Rule matches the declared testType — behaviour (with Outcome) for wire/live/e2e, invariant for arch/structure.
rubric.one-per-rule: Each Rule states exactly one guarantee, not a bundle or a restatement.
